audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 23, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for last-words genre, Spirit speech, just-rule standard, covenant confidence, thorn-burning rhetoric, warrior lists and variants, mass-killing boasts, land and food defense, dangerous leader desire, water poured as blood, foreign and regional identities, animal violence, and Uriah's final placement.

verses.restored: These are last words of David: declaration of David son of Jesse, declaration of man raised on high, anointed of Divine of Jacob, pleasant singer of songs of Israel.
Spirit of YHWH spoke through me, and YHWH's word was upon my tongue.
Divine of Israel said; Rock of Israel spoke to me: One ruling over humanity with justice, ruling in fear of Divine,
is like light of morning when sun rises, morning without clouds, when from brightness after rain grass springs from earth.
Is not my house thus with Divine? For an everlasting covenant YHWH set for me, ordered in all and guarded. For all my rescue and every desire—will YHWH not cause it to grow?
But worthless ones are all like thorns thrust away, because they cannot be taken by hand.
One who touches them arms oneself with iron and shaft of spear, and they are utterly burned with fire where they lie.
These are names of mighty warriors of David: Josheb-basshebeth the Tahchemonite, chief of Three; he wielded his spear against eight hundred slain at one time.
After him was Eleazar son of Dodo son of Ahohi, among three mighty warriors with David when they defied Philistines gathered there for battle and men of Israel went up/withdrew.
He arose and struck Philistines until his hand was weary and his hand clung to sword. YHWH made a great rescue that day, and people returned after him only to strip spoil.
After him was Shammah son of Agee the Hararite. Philistines gathered into a troop where there was a portion of field full of lentils, and people fled before Philistines.
He stationed himself in middle of portion, rescued it, and struck Philistines. YHWH made a great rescue.
Three from thirty chiefs went down and came at harvest to David at cave of Adullam, while a troop of Philistines camped in valley of Rephaim.
David was then in stronghold, and garrison of Philistines was then at Bethlehem.
David longed and said, Who will give me water to drink from well of Bethlehem that is by gate?
Three mighty warriors broke through camp of Philistines, drew water from well of Bethlehem by gate, carried it, and brought it to David. He was not willing to drink it but poured it out to YHWH.
He said, Far be it from me, YHWH, that I should do this. Is this not blood of men who went at risk of their lives? He was not willing to drink it. These things three mighty warriors did.
Abishai brother of Joab son of Zeruiah was chief of Thirty/Three. He wielded spear against three hundred slain and had a name among Three.
Was he not most honored of Thirty/Three and became their commander? Yet he did not attain to first Three.
Benaiah son of Jehoiada, son of a valiant man from Kabzeel, great in deeds: he struck two heroes/sons of Ariel of Moab; he also went down and struck a lion within pit on day of snow.
He struck an Egyptian man, impressive in appearance. Egyptian had spear in his hand, but Benaiah went down to him with staff, seized spear from Egyptian's hand, and killed him with his own spear.
These things Benaiah son of Jehoiada did and had a name among three mighty warriors.
He was honored more than thirty, but did not attain to Three. David placed him over his guard/council.
Asahel brother of Joab was among thirty; Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem,
Shammah the Harodite, Elika the Harodite,
Helez the Paltite, Ira son of Ikkesh the Tekoite,
Abiezer the Anathothite, Mebunnai/Sibbecai the Hushathite,
Zalmon the Ahohite, Maharai the Netophathite,
Heleb son of Baanah the Netophathite, Ittai son of Ribai from Gibeah of children of Benjamin,
Benaiah the Pirathonite, Hiddai from wadis of Gaash,
Abi-albon the Arbathite, Azmaveth the Barhumite,
Eliahba the Shaalbonite, sons of Jashen, Jonathan,
Shammah the Hararite, Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite,
Eliphelet son of Ahasbai son of Maacathite, Eliam son of Ahithophel the Gilonite,
Hezro the Carmelite, Paarai the Arbite,
Igal son of Nathan from Zobah, Bani the Gadite,
Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai the Beerothite, armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah,
Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite,
Uriah the Hittite—thirty-seven in all.

translation_notes: The 'last words' are a literary and theological collection, not necessarily David's final chronological speech.
The standard in verses 3-4 is justice under reverence for Divine. It judges David's reign; David's anointing does not prove he fulfilled it.
The everlasting covenant expresses royal confidence but cannot provide hereditary immunity, theocracy, supersessionism, or modern territorial entitlement.
The thorn-burning image dehumanizes opponents as disposable vegetation. It may not support execution, hell threats, political purges, or violence against people labeled worthless.
Warrior accounts preserve courage and community memory while also boasting hundreds of deaths. Casualty totals are not a holiness metric.
Eleazar's hand clinging to sword may reflect extreme exertion or injury. Trauma and bodily cost remain beneath the heroic summary.
Shammah's defense of a lentil field protects food and land, but the story still rests on killing combatants.

David's longing for Bethlehem water sends three people through lethal danger. His later refusal to drink recognizes their risk, but leaders are responsible for desires subordinates may interpret as commands.
Pouring the water as 'blood' refuses consumption of sacrifice offered for royal appetite; it does not erase the dangerous mission.
The list includes people from Judah, Benjamin, Ammon, Zobah, Gad, Beeroth, and other communities. Foreign identity does not reduce loyalty or humanity.
Benaiah's animal and human killings are reputation stories, not instructions for masculinity, animal cruelty, or ethnic superiority.
Uriah the Hittite closes the list. His name exposes the contradiction between David's celebrated warriors and the king who arranged one warrior's death.
safety_note: Do not turn the mighty-warrior catalogue into a masculinity test, weapon fantasy, or claim that high body counts prove Divine favor. Measure royal greatness by justice, protection of life, and accountability. Let Uriah's final name challenge every heroic summary that would forget the people power consumed.
